coredata.dc:title: Distinguish cancer cells based on targeting turn-on fluorescence imaging by folate functionalized green emitting carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

Xu, Shenghao
Niu, Caixia
Li, Minfeng
He, Dacheng
Lu, Zhonglin
Ma, Lin
Na, Na
Huang, Fu
Jiang, Hua
Ouyang, Jin
coredata.dc:description: Developing efficient methods for visual detection of cancer cells has the potential to contribute greatly to basic biological research and early diagnosis of cancer. Here, we report facile and one-step synthesis of green fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) with the help of a new passivating agent – poly(acrylate sodium) (PAAS). Based on the as-prepared CDs, a novel turn-on fluorescence probe was designed for targeting imaging of cancer cells via hydrogen-bond interaction between folic acid and CDs (FA-CDs). Intracellular experiments indicated that FA-CDs probe could accurately distinguish folate receptor (FR)-positive cancer cells in different cell mixtures with turn-on mode. In particular, combining the targeting of FA-CDs probe with the excellent photostability of CDs has inestimable meaning for fluorescence-assisted surgical resection and acquisition real-time information about tumor cells. Obviously, the as-prepared FA-CDs probe may have great potential as a high-performance platform for accurately recognizing special cancer cells, which may provide new tools for cancer prognosis and therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Distinguishing cancer cells
Turn-on fluorescence
Targeting imaging
Carbon dots
Green emitting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of the turn-on fluorescent probe
FA-CDs composites as turn-on fluorescence probe

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of the CDs. The inset shows the DLS result of the CDs. (B) UV–vis absorption (solid line) and fluorescent emission (dotted line) spectra of aqueous dispersion of the CDs. Inset: the photograph of CDs dispersion under UV light (365nm). (C) XPS, (D) C1s, and (E) O1s spectra of the CDs. (F) FT-IR spectrum of the CDs.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs in the presence of FA with different concentrations. (B) Plots of the values of (F 0−F)/F 0 at 520nm versus the concentration of FA. (C) Fluorescence “turn-on” of FA-CDs by FR. (D) Plots of the values of enhanced fluorescence intensity of the CDs at 520nm versus the concentration of FR. Inset: a linear range from 0.2 to 1.3µgmL−1 with a correlation coefficient R=0.99619. Excitation at 410nm. At a very low scanning speed.

Fluorescence images of (A) HeLa cells, (B) HepG2 cells, and (C) HEK-293 cells after incubation with the turn-on fluorescent probe (FA-CDs, 200µgmL−1) for 2h, respectively. Images acquired with blue light excitation; row 1: fluorescence images; row 2: bright-field images; row 3: merged images of the bright-field and fluorescence images. The scale bar of all the images is 50µm.

Fluorescence images of different cell mixtures with an almost-equal concentration of ~2×104 cellscm−2, respectively. The cell mixtures of (A) HeLa and HEK-293 cells and (B) HepG2 and HEK-293 cells incubated with FA-CDs probe (200µgmL−1) for 2h. Green fluorescence images of (A1) HeLa cells and (B1) HepG2 cells. Blue fluorescence images of cell nucleus of (A2) HeLa and HEK-293 cells and (B2) HepG2 and HEK-293 cells. White arrows indicate (A3) HeLa and (B3) HepG2 cells. The scale bar of all the images is 20µm.

Stability comparison of fluorescence signals from HeLa cells which are labeled with (A) CDs, (B) CdTe QDs and (C) FITC, respectively. Cell images are obtained by laser-scanning confocal microscopy; excitation at 405nm; the time in photograph means irradiation time. The scale bar of all the images is 20µm.
Scheme illustration of the designed fluorescence turn-on probe for imaging cancer cells overexpressed FR.

head.ce:title: Distinguish cancer cells based on targeting turn-on fluorescence imaging by folate functionalized green emitting carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Theoretical and Computational Photochemistry, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Theoretical and Computational Photochemistry, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry, Beijing Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory for Cell Proliferation and Regulation Biology, Ministry of Education, Beijing Normal University, Beijing 100875, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key laboratory for Cell Proliferation and Regulation Biology, Ministry of Education, Beijing Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences, CAS Key Laboratory of Photochemistry, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science, Beijing 100190, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences, CAS Key Laboratory of Photochemistry, Institute of Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Developing efficient methods for visual detection of cancer cells has the potential to contribute greatly to basic biological research and early diagnosis of cancer. Here, we report facile and one-step synthesis of green fluorescence carbon dots (CDs) with the help of a new passivating agent – poly(acrylate sodium) (PAAS). Based on the as-prepared CDs, a novel turn-on fluorescence probe was designed for targeting imaging of cancer cells via hydrogen-bond interaction between folic acid and CDs (FA-CDs). Intracellular experiments indicated that FA-CDs probe could accurately distinguish folate receptor (FR)-positive cancer cells in different cell mixtures with turn-on mode. In particular, combining the targeting of FA-CDs probe with the excellent photostability of CDs has inestimable meaning for fluorescence-assisted surgical resection and acquisition real-time information about tumor cells. Obviously, the as-prepared FA-CDs probe may have great potential as a high-performance platform for accurately recognizing special cancer cells, which may provide new tools for cancer prognosis and therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Realizing cancer cells detecting sensitively and specifically has significant meaning both for early prognosis and therapy, and researching metastasis of cancer (Chen et al., 2008; Lee et al., 2014; Yin et al., 2013). Traditional techniques including computed tomography, magnetic resonance imaging, and radionuclide imaging, require sophisticated instrument and are mainly fited for biological tissues (Feng et al., 2013). For cellular visual detection, fluorescence imaging has attracted considerable attention owing to its high sensitivity and spatiotemporal resolution (Chen et al., 2006; Zhang et al., 2012a). Recently, a few fluorescent probes for cancer cells detecting have been reported, for instance, by using fluorescent dyes or quantum dots (QDs) modified with targeting ligands to target specific cells (He et al., 2008; Qiao et al., 2013; Song et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2011). Nevertheless, most of these systems are always fluorescent, which not only needs many washing operations for accurate imaging of cells but also is not favorable to afford high signal/background ratio (Shi and Ma, 2012). Moreover, fluorescence always-on probe in the nontarget cells due to unspecific endocytosis can lead to a false positive signal (Zhang et al., 2012b). Comparable to always-on signals, an “on–off” or “off–on” switch mechanism should greatly help to address these issues and be more suitable for targeting cells detection because of enhancing optical spatial resolution (Bianying et al., 2013; Pan et al., 2013; Silvers et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2013).

Considerable efforts have been devoted to these fluorescence activated probes yet such probes are still rare (Mizusawa et al., 2012; Pan et al., 2013; Silvers et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2013). It is worth noting that these turn-on probes reported have two main problems: the preparation time- and labor-consuming and considerable toxicity from fluorescent reporters, which both limit the development of fluorescent activated probes for specific cells imaging and the advance of cancer diagnosis technology. Clearly, simplifying the work of fabricating the “turn-on” probes and selecting a fluorescent reporters with excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity are highly necessary.

In recent years, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention because of their fascinating physical properties, low toxicity and outstanding biocompatibility (Dong et al., 2012; Lu et al., 2012; Shi et al., 2013; Zong et al., 2014). In view of these exciting features, CDs have been widely explored as fluorescent markers or reporters for intracellular imaging (Jaiswal et al., 2012; Sahu et al., 2012; Xu et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2012). However, the fluorescence of these CDs is almost blue under maximum excitation in which there exists two tough problems for bio-imaging. First, blue light from carbohydrate in cells would interfere with the fluorescent signals from CDs (Wang et al., 2014). Then, biological tissues display higher absorption in blue fluorescence region than in longer-wavelength regions (Anderson and Parrish, 1983). Though the emitting of some CDs may produce red-shift when the excitation is in longer-wavelength, the intensity of the emitting decreases significantly. Considerable efforts have been devoted to shift the maximum emission light of CDs to longer-wavelength regions (Fang et al., 2011; Wang et al., 2014), but these works exist some problems, such as low quantum yield (QY), complex syntheses procedure, and using toxic components. Moreover, almost all the probes based on CDs for cell imaging are always fluorescent (Song et al., 2012; Xu et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2012). Now, it is still a challenge to develop an turn-on fluorescence probe for distinguishing cancer cells based on longer-wavelength emitting CDs.

Herein, we report a new passivating agent – poly(acrylate sodium) (PAAS) for facile synthesis of CDs with strong green fluorescence. With the as-prepared CDs, the folic acid-CDs (FA-CDs) nanocomposite was designed as a turn-on fluorescence probe for targeting imaging of cancer cells overexpressed folate receptor (FR) with a negligible fluorescence background. The general design strategy is shown in Scheme 1. The way of fabricating probe is via a kind of noncovalent bond – hydrogen-bond, which can be applied widely and provide a new direction for preparing turn-on fluorescence probes. Moreover, not only the FA-CDs probe can target specific cancer cells for imaging but also the activated fluorescence of FA-CDs probe has the excellent photostability, which has significance for fluorescence-assisted surgical resection and acquisition real-time information about tumor cells. In view of these advantages, the as-prepared FA-CDs probe may have great potential as a high-performance platform for recognizing special cancer cells via fluorescence turn-on imaging, which may provide a new tool for cancer diagnosis and therapy.

PAAS and Glucose (Glu) purchased from Sigma-Aldrich were used to prepare the CDs. Poly(methacrylate sodium) (PMAS), Polyacrylamide (PAM), 3-(4,5-dimethylthiahizol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT), fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC), FA, and FR were also obtained from Sigma-Aldrich. The HeLa human cancer of cervix cell line, HepG2 human hepatic carcinoma cell line, HEK-293 human embryonic kidney 293 cell line, Dulbecco′s modified eagle media (DMEM), minimum essential medium (MEM), fetal bovine serum (FBS), and Penicillin–Streptomycin Solution (PSS) were obtained from Peking Union Medical College Hospital. All other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solutions with required pH values were obtained with NaOH solution (1molL−1) titrating H3PO4 solution (0.01molL−1). All the experiments were carried out with ultrapure water.

CDs were synthesized by microwave assisted hydrothermal carbonization the mixture of Glu and PAAS. In a typical procedure, 2g Glu and 1g PAAS were dissolved with 10mL water and then heated with microwave (300W) for 4min. The prepared CDs solution is brownish yellow and stored at 4°C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the turn-on fluorescent probe
ce:section.ce:para: FA (1mL, 2mgmL−1) was added into 1mL of the CDs stock solution and then mixed fully. After being set for 10min, the mixture was dialyzed against PBS (0.01M, pH 7.4) for 2h thrice to remove superfluous FA. The purified solution of FA-CDs was keeped in the dark place for further experiments.

In the experiment of isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC), the reaction cell was totally filled with FA solution and the titration injector syringe was also full filled with the CDs. During the titration, the CDs was titrated to FA with 30 injections of 10µL each spaced 2min apart.

MTT assay was carried out to evaluate cellular toxicity with HeLa cells. HeLa cells were seeded in a 96-well plate for 24h to adhere. Then, serial dilutions of CDs with known concentrations were added into cells for incubation of another 24h. According to the formula, the relative viabilities of cells were calculated with absorption values at 490nm. Three independent experiments were performed under identical conditions. The cytotoxicity of PAAS was obtained following the same operations as described above.

Three cell lines, HeLa, HepG2, and HEK-293, were used for fluorescence imaging. The HeLa and HepG2 cells were cultured in a DMEM medium with 10% FBS and 0.01% PSS, and HEK-293 cells were cultured in an MEM medium with 10% FBS and 0.01% PSS at 37°C in a 5% CO2 incubator. For the in vitro imaging studies, cells were incubated in a confocal dish 24h to adhere, and then the CDs were added to dish for incubating another 4h under standard conditions. After being washed with PBS (0.01M, pH 7.4) three times, the cells were imaged.

In the experiments of co-incubation, HEK-293 cells were first incubated for 4h in confocal dish, and then HeLa cells with an almost-equal concentration (~ 2×104 cellscm−2) were added into dish to co-culture with HEK-293 cells for another 8h. Then, the cell mixtures were incubated with FA-CDs probe (200µgmL−1) for 2h, and were stained with Hoechst 33342 (5µgmL−1) for 30min in succession at 37°C. Fluorescence imaging of cell mixture of HepG2 and HEK-293 cells was obtained following the same operations as described above.

The morphology of the CDs was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). It is can be seen that the CDs have a uniform dispersion without any apparent aggregation ( Fig. 1A). The average size of CDs was 3.5nm measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) (inset in Fig. 1A). The UV–vis absorption (solid curve) and fluorescent emission spectra (dotted curve) of CDs are shown in Fig. 1B. From the UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, a strong peak at 280nm was observed, which is reported to be attributed to π–π⁎ transition of aromatic sp2 domains (Ananthanarayanan et al., 2014; Li et al., 2012). When excited at 410nm a strong emission peak centered at 520nm is shown in the fluorescent spectra and QY of CDs determined with quinine sulfate (0.1M H2SO4 as solvent; QY=0.55) as a standard was ~5.4%. Inset in Fig. 1B shows the photograph of CDs, which is obtained under UV light (365nm). The intense green color of the photograph further confirms that the CDs have strong green fluorescence. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1C) shows two peaks at 285.0 and 532.6eV, which can be ascribed to C1s and O1s, respectively (Lu et al., 2012). The C1s spectrum (Fig. 1D) revealed that the surface of the CDs has three groups, sp2 C=C (284.8eV), C=O/CO (282.6eV) and OC=O (289.2eV) (Liu et al., 2011b). The CDs also have the other two groups, C=O (531.7eV) and COC (533.4eV), which can be seen in O1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) (Liu et al., 2011a). The FTIR analysis of the CDs (Fig. 1F) further revealed that there are two main functional groups, COOH and COH, in the surface of the CDs, with the fact: asymmetric and symmetric streching vibration peaks of COO at 1600 and 1400cm−1 and characteristic absorption of COH streching at 1272cm−1 (Krishnakumar and Balachandran, 2004; Lu et al., 2012). Because of these functional groups, the as-prepared CDs have excellent hydrophilicity and stability in aqueous systems (Berlin et al., 2010, 2011). Furthermore, ionic strength, pH and exposure under UV have little influence on fluorescent stability of the CDs as shown in Fig. S1. All these features show that the as-prepared CDs are good candidates for biological and biomedical applications. Additionally, we investigated the effects of the syntheses conditions on fluorescence intensity of the CDs, such as the power of microwave, PAAS-to-Glu ratio and even passivating reagent (Fig. S2), which were identified as the critical factors in the synthesis of high fluorescent CDs. In addition, we evaluated the cytotoxicity of PAAS by MTT assay using HeLa cells as shown in Fig. S3, which indicated that PAAS possessed low cytotoxicity. Since the cytotoxicity of CDs is largely similar to its free surface passivation molecule (Wang et al., 2011a), PAAS can act as a good passivation agent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FA-CDs composites as turn-on fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: FA, a typical cell-targeting agent, has high affinity to FR which is known to be overexpressed by many human cancer cells but rarely expressed in normal cells (Yin et al., 2013; Zhang et al., 2012b). As shown in Fig. 2A, fluorescence intensity of CDs at 520nm gradually decrease with an increase FA concentration, which indicated FA could significantly quench the fluorescence of the CDs. When the concentration of FA is high, the decrease of CDs fluorescence intensity becomes slowly (Fig. 2B), which suggests the surface of CDs have been saturated with FA, and thus the formation of FA-CDs nanocomposite.

To make certain the mechanism of the fluorescence quenching of CDs by FA, we in-depth studied interaction between FA and CDs. The Zeta potential of CDs and FA were −0.07 and −41.3mV (Fig. S4A and B), respectively, signifying that the combination of CDs with FA is not by the way of electrostatic interaction. The surface of CDs has abundant OH and COOH functional groups and FA molecule (Fig. S5) also has NH2, OH and COOH groups, implying that the CDs could form high hydrogen-bond interaction with FA. A further study was carried out by (ITC) to understand the interaction between CDs and FA. Fig. S6 shows the ITC profile for the binding of FA to CDs. In this assay, ΔH<0 (−247.3kcalmol−1), and ΔS>0 (8.58calmol−1 deg−1), they indicate that the interaction of the CDs with FA is hydrogen-bond and van der waals interaction with a weak hydrophobic force (Ross and Subramanian, 1981). Fig. S7 shows that UV–vis absorption of the FA-CDs nanocomposite is consistent with FA and CDs, which demonstrate FA has absorbed on the surface of CDs successfully. To gain more insight into the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the fluorescent decay lifetime of CDs in the absence and presence of FA were examined. The average fluorescence lifetime decreases significantly from 6.2ns of CDs to 1.7ns of FA-CDs nanocomposite (Fig. S8), indicating that there is an fast electron-transfer process between CDs and FA and fluorescence quenching of CDs is dynamic (Zhu et al., 2013). Furthermore, the method of calibration curve was applied to determine the amount of FA binding on CDs. As shown in Fig. S9, the absorbance of FA with a series of concentrations (A), used to obtain the calibration curve (B), and the absorbance of FA-CDs with a known concentration of CDs (C), were measured, which were used to figure out 0.197µg FA binding with 1µg CDs in the solution of FA-CDs.

FA has stronger interaction with FR than CDs because of the high affinity of FA for FR (Zhang et al., 2012b). In theory, when FR is added to the solution of FA-CDs nanocomposite fluorescence of the CDs would recover owing to FA desorbing from the surface of CDs and binding with FR. As expected, the fluorescence intensity of FA-CDs nanocomposite solution increased with the concentration of added FR (Fig. 2C). The fluorescence enhancing data in Fig. 2D does not fit a linear relationship in the whole concentration range. And a linear range was from 0.2 to 1.3µgmL−1 with a correlation coefficient R=0.99619 (inset in Fig. 2D). To evaluate the selectivity of FA-CDs probe for FR, we studied the fluorescent response of the probe toward to some common proteins, amino acids, and polypeptides, each at a concentration of 10µgmL−1, (Fig. S10). While FR could enhance the fluorescence of FA-CDs probe significantly, other biological molecules did not cause an obvious fluorescence change. The above results indicate that the FA-CDs probe has high selectivity for FR, which coincide to the fact that FA is a high-affinity ligand to FR (Zhang et al., 2012b).

For further biological applications, MTT assays were first carried out to evaluate the cytotoxicity of CDs using the HeLa cell line (Fig. S11), which showed that the CDs possess low cytotoxicity and hence could apply to biological system. To assess the feasibility of the prepared fluorescence turn-on probe for recognition cancer cells with overexpression of FR, HeLa cells (human cancer cells with overexpression of FR), HepG2 cells (human cancer cells with a high-expression of FR), and HEK-293 cells (human normal cells with negative FR), were used for experiment of cell targeting imaging. As shown in Fig. 3A, bright fluorescence can be seen in HeLa cells after a 2h incubation with the FA-CDs probe because of the fluorescence recovery of the probe. While negligible fluorescence was observed in normal HEK-293 cells low-expressed FR (Fig. 3C), indicating that FA remained adsorbing on the surface of CDs during the endocytosis process. Fig. 3B shows that fluorescence brightness of HepG2 cells was in the middle order among the three cell lines. However, when HeLa and HepG2 cells were first incubated with excess free FA and then FA-CDs probe was added under the same conditions, the free FA would block FR on two kinds of cancer cells. Consequently, no fluorescence was observed in these cells as shown in Fig. S12. As a control, three types of cells were incubated only with CDs, and they all became brightly illuminated with green images (Fig. S13), which indicated that the CDs could rapidly deliver into the cells and maintain its high fluorescence.

To confirm the potential of FA-CDs probe for distinguishing FR-positive cancer cells in cell mixture with normal cells, when FR-positive cancer cells and normal cells coexisted, the co-incubation experiment of HeLa/HepG2 cells and HEK-293 cells was performed. The size of HeLa and HepG2 cells are both larger than HEK-293 cells, which can help to conveniently verify the results of cell imaging. Note that a longer co-culturing time such as 24h may lead HEK-293 cells to float because of interference from other co-culturing cells and its poor ability of adherence. The solution we adopted was that HEK-293 cells were allowed to incubate for 4h first in confocal dish, and then HeLa/HepG2 cells were added into dish to co-culture with HEK-293 cells for another 8h. In order to clearly show the location of the cells, three types of cells were post-treated with Hoechst 33342 (a fluorescent dye) to stain nuclei for 30min. As depicted in Fig. 4, the cell mixtures of HeLa/HepG2 and HEK-293 cells were incubated with the probe for 2h, a intense green fluorescence was only observed in HeLa cells (images A1) and HepG2 cells shown weaker fluorescent signal (images B1) while HEK-293 cells did not. The difference in fluorescent signal obviously is consistent with the fact that HeLa cells overexpress FR and HepG2 cells highexpress FR, while HEK-293 cells lack FR, therefore demonstrating capacity of the FA-CDs probe of identifying FR-positive cancer cells in cell mixture with normal cells.

As we all know, combining the targeting with long-term fluorescence imaging has inestimable meaning for fluorescence-assisted surgical resection and obtaining real-time information about tumor cells. Thus, we investigated the ability of as-prepared CDs for long-term cellular imaging. As shown in Fig. 5A, the green signals of the CDs are still intense after 120min continuous irradiation with laser-scanning confocal microscopy, owing to the excellent photostability of the CDs. Using the CdTe QDs and FITC as fluorescent probe as a control, the signals became very weak soon under irradiation (Fig. 5B and C). As expected, after FA-CDs probes targeting HeLa cells, the turn-on fluorescence also hardly became weak during 2h continuous observation (Fig. S14). Furthermore, we studied the dynamic process of the fluorescence turn-on of CDs on HeLa cells at different time points. As shown in Fig. S15, the fluorescence gradually became intense from 0 to 2h, which indicated that the CDs were released and delivered into cells at the presence of FR overexpressed on the HeLa cells. However, owing to the continuous 2h exposure, the intensity of the fluorescence shown in Fig. S15E was weaker than that shown in Fig. 3A. And the fluorescence would weaken (Fig. S15F) with the observation going on. This phenomenon also proves that the FA-CDs probe can detect cancer cells with a turn-on mode and the CDs have excellent photostability. These results confirm that the FA-CDs probe could act as a turn-on fluorescent probe at the cellular level and then have the potential to recognize specific cancer cell types that express FR.

In this work, we use a new passivating agent – PAAS for facile and one-step synthesis of green fluorescence CDs. Then, with the prepared CDs we designed an activated fluorescent probe, FA-CDs, for fluorescence turn-on imaging of cancer cells. The proposed FA-CDs probe is suited for distinguishing FR-positive cancer cells in cell mixture with normal cells. With the capacity of targeting and excellent photostability of the activated fluorescence, the FA-CDs probe would have great potential significance as a high-performance platform for fluorescence-assisted surgical resection. Our work possess some remarkable significance: facile synthesis of green fluorescence CDs, simple and rapid preparation turn-on fluorescence probe for specific cancer cells detection, and the FA-CDs probe possessing not only targeting but also excellent photostability of its activated fluorescence, which may provide a new direction for determining prognosis of cancer and investigation of cancer metastasis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21175014, 21475011) and National Grant of Basic Research Program of China (No. 2011CB915504).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2014.08.052.
